title: $100B Climate Finance Goal
description: Developed nations' pledge to mobilize $100 billion per year in climate finance for developing countries.
summary: Developed countries first pledged $100 billion per year by 2020 at Copenhagen in 2009. The goal was missed in 2020 and 2021, finally exceeded at $115.9 billion in 2022. OECD confirmed it was met three years running: $132.8 billion in 2023, $136.7 billion in 2024. At COP29, nations agreed a new target: $300 billion per year by 2035, with a broader aspiration of $1.3 trillion from all sources. Developing nations called it wholly inadequate.
insight: The $100B goal was met three years late, then exceeded. But support for low-income countries actually declined to $8.4B in 2023. The headline hides the distribution.

stats.label: 2024 mobilised
stats.context: third consecutive year exceeding the goal

stats.label: New COP29 goal by 2035
stats.context: from developed nations; $1.3T aspiration from all sources

title: EU CBAM Definitive Phase
description: The EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism enters its definitive phase with financial liability for importers.
summary: The world's first carbon border tax went live on January 1, 2026. EU importers of cement, aluminium, fertilisers, iron and steel, hydrogen, and electricity must now purchase CBAM certificates priced to the EU ETS carbon market. The European Commission published the first quarterly certificate price at €75.36 per tonne of CO2 for Q1 2026. Russia filed a formal WTO dispute in May 2025; the EU declined to enter consultations.
insight: CBAM turns carbon leakage policy into a real import cost for EU supply chains, not just a reporting regime.

stats.label: Sectors covered
stats.context: cement, aluminium, fertilisers, iron & steel, hydrogen, electricity

stats.label: Q1 2026 certificate price
stats.context: calculated from EU ETS auction clearing prices

stats.label: WTO disputes filed
stats.context: formal request by Russia, May 2025; EU declined consultations

title: US Paris Withdrawal
description: The United States officially withdraws from the Paris Agreement for the second time.
summary: President Trump signed the executive order to exit the Paris Agreement on January 20, 2025, his first day back in office. The formal withdrawal took effect on January 27, 2026. In February 2026, the US went further: notifying the UN of its withdrawal from the UNFCCC itself, the first country ever to do so. Twenty-four US states and territories maintain Paris commitments through the US Climate Alliance.
insight: The US has left Paris twice in ten years. Now it's withdrawing from the UNFCCC entirely. 14% of global emissions have no federal climate mandate and no seat at the table.

stats.label: States maintaining Paris
stats.context: US Climate Alliance members as of 2026

title: 43% Global Emissions Reduction
description: Global greenhouse gas emissions must decline by 43% from 2019 levels to align with 1.5°C pathways.
summary: The IPCC's most critical near-term benchmark: a 43% reduction in global greenhouse gas emissions from 2019 levels by 2030 to keep the 1.5°C target viable. Global emissions hit a record 57.7 GtCO2e in 2024, growing 2.6% year-on-year. Current NDCs, even if fully implemented, project 2.3-2.5°C of warming. Only 60 countries representing 63% of global emissions have submitted new 2035 pledges.
insight: Current policies will deliver a fraction of the 43% cut needed. Emissions hit a new record in 2024. The gap has never been wider.

stats.label: 2024 global emissions
stats.context: CO2e, a new record high (+2.6% YoY)

title: Global Net-Zero Target
description: Mid-century target for 150+ countries to achieve net-zero greenhouse gas emissions.
summary: Over 150 countries covering roughly 90% of global emissions have made net-zero pledges for around 2050. As of 2026, 35 countries have targets enshrined in law (50 including EU member states). The remaining commitments are policy documents, political declarations, or proposals. Even if all pledges are fulfilled, the IEA estimates 22 billion tonnes of CO2 would remain in 2050, consistent with roughly 2.1°C of warming.
insight: 150+ countries have pledged net-zero. Only 35 have made it law. And even full delivery leaves 22 Gt of CO2 in 2050, consistent with 2.1°C.
